---
title: "Getting Started with Quarto"
description: "An introduction to Quarto — the modern successor to R Markdown — covering how to create documents that mix text and code, control output rendering, and produce HTML and PDF reports."
author: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
date: 2026-06-16
date-modified: 2026-06-16
image: ../../assets/images/placeholder.png

learning-objectives:
  - "Create a Quarto document that combines prose and R code chunks"
  - "Control code and output visibility using chunk options (echo, eval, include)"
  - "Render a Quarto document to HTML with a table of contents and a custom theme"
  - "Render a Quarto document to PDF"
  - "Share a rendered HTML document online using Netlify Drop"

categories:
  - R
  - quarto
  - reproducibility
  - BA
  - tutorial

level:
  - BA

content-type: tutorial

citation:
  type: entry
  container-title: "Methods Hub"
  title: "Getting Started with Quarto"
  editor:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  url: https://euf-methodology-hub.netlify.app/resources/r-programming/getting-started-with-quarto
---

Quarto is a modern, multi-language successor to *R Markdown*. The core idea is simple:
write text and code in the same document, render it, and get a polished report, paper,
or website. Quarto handles the conversion — you focus on the content.

This website is itself written in Quarto, which gives you a sense of what is possible
with the tool even for everyday writing tasks.

# Video lecture {.unnumbered}

```{=html}
<iframe
  width="560" height="315"
  src="https://www.youtube-nocookie.com/embed/videoseries?si=Ts2M1v35TDmtfbr3&amp;list=PLZDawQMrG1RLKUDufKf8EJz4Kj4WC_Kco"
  title="YouTube video player"
  frameborder="0"
  allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share"
  referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
  allowfullscreen>
</iframe>
```

# Required reading

Work through these in order — they cover everything you need to get started:

1. [The CommonMark markdown tutorial](https://commonmark.org/help/tutorial/) — markdown
   is the text-formatting language inside every Quarto document; this interactive tutorial
   takes about 10 minutes
2. [Quarto tutorial I: the basics](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/hello/rstudio.html)
3. [Quarto tutorial II: computations](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/computations/rstudio.html)
4. [Quarto tutorial III: authoring](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/authoring/rstudio.html)

# Exercise

Create a new Quarto document where you set the title, date, and author explicitly.
Write a sample text that includes:

- at least one level-1 heading
- at least two level-2 headings
- a YAML option that hides R code by default
- one R chunk where both the code and output are shown
- one R chunk that produces a ggplot2 figure, with the code hidden

Then do the following:

1. Render to HTML with a floating table of contents and a
   [custom theme](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-themes.html).
2. Publish the HTML via [Netlify Drop](https://app.netlify.com/drop) and add a download
   link for the source file.
   *(The HTML file must be named `index.html` for Netlify Drop to work.)*
3. Render to PDF and include a table of contents.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Sample solution
A sample solution is available as a [GitHub Gist](https://gist.github.com/graebnerc/f42962b9ecbf9d7a7e6dc5593be691bd).
The rendered Netlify version is [here](https://quarto-ex-solution.netlify.app/).
:::

You can also practice with the interactive `Quarto` exercise set from the
`DataScienceExercises` package (see the [blog post](../../blog/2026-06-16-datascienceexercises/index.qmd)
on how to install and run it):

```{r, eval=FALSE}
learnr::run_tutorial(
  name = "Quarto",
  package = "DataScienceExercises",
  shiny_args = list("launch.browser" = TRUE)
)
```

# Further reading

- [We don't talk about Quarto](https://www.apreshill.com/blog/2022-04-we-dont-talk-about-quarto/) — a readable blog post introducing Quarto
- [Quarto and R Markdown](https://yihui.org/en/2022/04/quarto-r-markdown/) — context on the transition from R Markdown
- [Markdown basics](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html) — the full Quarto markdown reference
- [The comprehensive Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/)
- [The R Markdown Cookbook](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown-cookbook/) — still relevant for understanding the ecosystem
